name: OccField Manager (Complete Example)
description: Fully populated example showing what a comprehensive knowledge base looks like for an HQMC OccField Manager billet.

billet.title: OccField Manager, 0602
billet.billetDescription: Manage the health and development of the 0602 Communications occupational field. Responsibilities include T/O management, school seat allocation, MOS Manual maintenance, monitor coordination, and career path development for all 06XX Marines.

narratives.prompt: What is the primary mission of your billet, and how does it contribute to your unit's overall mission?
narratives.response: The primary mission of this billet is to manage the health and development of the 0602 Communications occupational field across the entire Marine Corps. This means I am the single person at HQMC responsible for ensuring the right number of Marines with the right skills exist in the right grades to fill every 06XX billet on every T/O in the force. If this billet goes vacant for 30 days, MOS Manual changes stall, school seat negotiations with TECOM lose their advocate, billet change requests stack up without OccField concurrence, and the M&RA monitor loses their primary coordination partner for assignment decisions. Within PP&O, this billet feeds the Future Operations Branch's ability to project future force structure needs for the communications community. Every decision about whether to create, merge, or delete a communications MOS flows through this desk. The billet is the connective tissue between what the fleet needs and what the institutional Marine Corps can produce and sustain.

narratives.prompt: Describe a typical week in this billet. What are the recurring meetings, deadlines, and touchpoints?
narratives.response: Monday starts with the POF Branch standup at 0800 where each OccField manager gives a 2-minute status update on active issues. Tuesday morning is the weekly call with the M&RA monitor at MPP-40 -- this is the most important recurring event on the calendar and should never be skipped or delegated. Wednesday is generally open for analytical work: pulling TFDW data, building slides for upcoming briefs, or drafting staffing packages. Thursday morning I pull the weekly manning snapshot from TFDW and cross-reference it against the monitor's tracker to catch any discrepancies early. Thursday afternoon is the PP&O front office read-ahead deadline -- any slides or decision papers due to DirOps the following week must be submitted by 1500. Friday is flex for catch-up, but once a month the DirOps holds a formal OccField health review where two or three managers brief their quarterly data. Know your rotation date and start building slides at least two weeks out.

narratives.prompt: Who are the three to five people or offices you interact with most frequently, and what do you work with them on?
narratives.response: The most critical relationship is with the 06XX monitor at M&RA (MPP-40). This person controls assignment decisions for every Marine in your OccField, and if you lose this relationship, you lose your ability to influence where talent goes. Second is the TECOM Training Pipeline Manager at Quantico who manages school seat quotas and course throughput -- without this partnership, your training pipeline analysis is guesswork. Third is your Branch Chief in POF, who is your direct supervisor and the gateway to DirOps for any decision that requires PP&O front office involvement. Fourth is the Communications School Director at Twentynine Palms (MCCES), who runs the actual schoolhouse and can tell you ground truth about instructor shortages, curriculum issues, and student quality that the data systems miss. Fifth is the G-3 communications planner at each MEF, who represents the operational demand signal and provides the fleet feedback that justifies your recommendations to leadership.

narratives.prompt: What are the most important processes or SOPs you manage or execute? Where are they documented?
narratives.response: The four core processes are: (1) MOS Manual change process governed by MCO 1200.17 -- the change template and tracking spreadsheet are on the PP&O SharePoint under OccField Manager Resources; (2) T/O Change Request (TOCR) concurrence process, where the SOP is in the POF Branch SOP binder and the tracking spreadsheet is on the shared drive at S:\POF\OccField\TOCR_Tracker.xlsx; (3) Quarterly MOS Health Report, which follows the DirOps brief template stored on SharePoint -- the data pull procedures are documented in a Word file I created at S:\POF\OccField\0602\Data_Pull_SOP.docx; and (4) Annual Training Input Plan (TIP) submission to TECOM, due each March, with the template provided by TECOM and archived on the shared drive. The informal but critical process is the weekly monitor sync -- there is no SOP for this, but the rhythm and agenda format are captured in my Outlook recurring appointment notes. I strongly recommend keeping those notes current for your successor.

narratives.prompt: What are the biggest 'gotchas' or lessons learned that a new person in this billet needs to know immediately?
narratives.response: First and most important: TFDW data lags by 60 to 90 days. If you brief TFDW numbers as current inventory to DirOps, you will be wrong and you will lose credibility. Always cross-reference with the monitor's live tracker. Second: never skip the informal pre-coordination step on MOS Manual changes. The formal staffing process is slow enough already -- if you blindside M&RA or TECOM with a change they have not seen, they will object on principle and you lose months. Third: budget narrative season sneaks up on you. The POM cycle submission deadlines are not on the PP&O calendar by default -- you need to track them yourself through the Comptroller's office. Miss the window and your funding request waits another year. Fourth: the T/O Review Board meets quarterly, but the submission deadline is 6 weeks before the board. If you miss the deadline, your TOCR waits 3-4 months. Fifth: the DirOps does not want to be surprised. If something is going sideways with an MOS or a training pipeline, tell your Branch Chief immediately so they can give DirOps a heads-up before it appears in a data brief from M&RA.

narratives.prompt: What decisions have you made that your successor should understand the reasoning behind?
narratives.response: The biggest decision was recommending the merger of MOS 0621 (Field Radio Operator) and 0623 (MAGTF Planner, Communications) into the consolidated 0671 (Data Systems Administrator) track. This was controversial because it eliminated two legacy MOSs with strong community identity, but the data was clear: both MOSs had chronic understrength at the mid-grade level because the skill sets were converging and Marines were laterally moving to 0671 anyway. By consolidating, we created a single healthy pipeline instead of three anemic ones. I also decided to non-concur on II MEF's request for eight additional 0699 billets on the MEF headquarters T/O because the MOS was already at 115% fill and adding billets would have masked the real problem, which was grade structure imbalance. Finally, I pushed to increase school seat quotas for 0631 by 15% based on projected EAS losses, over TECOM's initial objection that they lacked instructor capacity. The compromise was a phased increase with TECOM hiring two additional instructors.

narratives.prompt: What are the active issues or ongoing projects that need immediate attention from your successor?
narratives.response: Three items need attention within the first 30 days. First: the 0612 Field Wireman school seat shortage is critical -- we are 30% under quota this fiscal year due to an instructor billet that has been gapped for five months. I have a package in staffing to TECOM requesting emergency fill authorization, and it needs follow-up by mid-May or it falls off their priority list. Second: the FY27 POM submission for communications MOS restructuring is due to the Comptroller by 1 June. The narrative is drafted but needs final data validation and Branch Chief review. The file is on the shared drive. Third: the annual OccField symposium is scheduled for September and planning must start in earnest by June. The venue reservation at the National Conference Center is confirmed but the concept paper has not been routed yet. Beyond 30 days, the next quarterly MOS Health Report is due to DirOps in July, and the annual Training Input Plan coordination with TECOM begins in January but requires pre-work starting in November.

narratives.prompt: What annual or periodic events does this billet manage or participate in? When do they occur and what preparation is needed?
narratives.response: The annual calendar is anchored by four major events. January through March: Training Input Plan (TIP) development and submission to TECOM. Start pulling pipeline data in November; the formal submission is due in March. April and October: Quarterly MOS Health Reports to DirOps. Begin data pulls three weeks prior and pre-brief the Branch Chief one week before. June: POM submission window. Budget narratives for any manpower or training funding requests must be finalized and submitted through the PP&O Comptroller. September: OccField Symposium. Planning starts at T-6 months (March), with invitations out by May and logistics locked by July. Additionally, the T/O Review Board meets quarterly (Jan, Apr, Jul, Oct) with submission deadlines six weeks prior. The MOS Manual annual review cycle runs October through February with the updated manual published in March. Finally, the Commandant's Planning Guidance and Force Design updates may generate ad-hoc taskings at any time -- these are unpredictable but always high priority.

narratives.prompt: What systems, tools, or accounts does this billet require access to? Are there any pending access requests?
narratives.response: You need access to the following systems, and several have long lead times so start the access requests on day one. TFDW (Total Force Data Warehouse) is your primary data source -- access request goes through DMDC and takes 2-3 weeks. MCTFS (Marine Corps Total Force System) for individual Marine records -- CAC-enabled access but you need a role request through M&RA that takes 1-2 weeks. The PP&O SharePoint site has all templates, trackers, and branch SOPs -- your Branch Chief can grant access immediately. The TECOM Information Management System (TECMIS) provides school seat and training pipeline data -- request through TECOM G-6 and expect 3-4 weeks. You also need to be added to several Outlook distribution lists: PP&O-POF-All, OccField-Managers, and the 06XX-Community list. Request through the Branch admin. Finally, request a SIPR token and SIPR email account if you do not already have one -- some joint billet coordination requires classified communication. The SIPR request takes 4-6 weeks and is the longest lead item.

narratives.prompt: What would you do differently if you were starting this billet over again? What advice would you give your successor?
narratives.response: I would spend my entire first two weeks doing nothing but building relationships. I dove straight into the data and processes when I arrived, and it took me three months to realize that this billet runs on relationships, not spreadsheets. Go visit the M&RA monitor in person during week one -- do not email, do not call, walk over to their building and introduce yourself. Fly to Twentynine Palms in the first month to meet the schoolhouse leadership face-to-face. Call the MEF G-3 comm planners and introduce yourself. These relationships are the foundation of everything you will do. Second, I would set up my data pull routine immediately and automate as much as possible. I wasted weeks early on manually pulling the same reports. Third, attend every DirOps brief you can in the first month, even if your OccField is not on the agenda. You need to understand DirOps's priorities and communication style before you brief them yourself. Fourth, read every symposium after-action report from the last three years. The institutional history is invaluable and keeps you from re-litigating settled issues.

narratives.prompt: Are there any political dynamics, sensitivities, or relationship nuances that your successor should be aware of?
narratives.response: The single biggest political dynamic is the tension between PP&O and M&RA on fill priorities. PP&O sets force structure requirements, M&RA executes assignments, and the two organizations do not always agree on priorities. When there are not enough Marines to fill every billet, PP&O wants critical billets filled first while M&RA balances assignment feasibility, PCS costs, and Marine preferences. You will find yourself in the middle of this tension constantly. Always maintain credibility with both sides by using data, not opinions. Within PP&O, the different OccField managers sometimes compete for DirOps attention and resources. Do not get drawn into turf battles -- focus on your field's data and let the numbers speak. Externally, the MEFs each believe their requirements should be prioritized over the others. Be scrupulously even-handed in how you distribute school seats and respond to billet requests. If a MEF CG calls the DC PP&O to complain that you are favoring another MEF, you have a serious problem. Finally, TECOM is sensitive about being told how to run their schoolhouses. Frame everything as a request for partnership, not a directive.

narratives.prompt: What resources, references, or reading materials would you recommend for someone new to this billet?
narratives.response: Start with MCO 1200.17 (MOS Manual) -- read the sections relevant to the 06XX field cover to cover. Then read the current Commandant's Planning Guidance and Force Design Annual Update for context on where the Marine Corps is heading, because that directly affects which MOSs grow, shrink, or transform. The PP&O OccField Manager Handbook on SharePoint is outdated but still useful for understanding the formal processes. Read the last three OccField symposium after-action reports archived on the shared drive -- these contain the community's own assessment of their problems and priorities. The M&RA Enlisted Assignments SOP explains how the monitor actually makes assignment decisions, which helps you understand their constraints. TECOM's Training and Education Master Plan gives you the institutional perspective on training pipeline management. For data analysis, the TFDW User Guide is essential. Finally, I keep a personal reference binder with annotated copies of every MOS Manual change I processed, every DirOps brief I gave, and every significant staffing action. It is in the file cabinet behind the desk and I recommend your successor continue maintaining it.

narratives.prompt: What are the key health indicators for your occupational field, and which ones are you most concerned about?
narratives.response: The key health indicators I track are fill rate by MOS and grade, first-term retention rate, lateral move rates in and out, school seat utilization and attrition, and time-to-train for each MOS-producing course. Right now my biggest concern is the 0612 Field Wireman community, which is 30% under quota on school seats this fiscal year due to a gapped instructor billet at MCCES. If we do not fix this in the next 60 days, the FY27 inventory projection shows the MOS dropping below 80% fill at the E-4/E-5 level, which triggers a manning crisis across the MEFs. Second concern is the post-merger health of 0671 after absorbing 0621 and 0623 Marines. The consolidation is on paper but the retraining pipeline is just starting, and early attrition data from the bridge course is higher than projected at 18% versus the 10% we planned for. Third, 0699 is overmanned at the SNCO level by about 15%, which creates a promotion bottleneck that is starting to affect retention at the Sergeant level as Marines see limited upward mobility.

narratives.prompt: How does the relationship with the monitor work, and what does the next person need to know about that dynamic?
narratives.response: The monitor relationship is the single most important professional relationship in this billet. The current 06XX monitor at MPP-40 is a GySgt with 14 years in the field who knows the community deeply. We have a standing Tuesday morning call at 0900 where we review the week's assignment actions, discuss any problem fills, and align on priority billets. The dynamic works well when both sides share information freely -- I give the monitor advance notice of pending billet changes or MOS Manual updates that will affect assignments, and the monitor gives me early warning on Marines who are being flagged for separation, pending legal holds, or unusual assignment requests. The key friction point is when PP&O and M&RA disagree on fill priorities. In those cases, the monitor and I have an informal agreement to present a joint recommendation rather than contradicting each other at the staff level. Never go around the monitor to their leadership -- if you have a disagreement, resolve it at the working level or escalate through your own chain. If a new monitor rotates in, invest heavily in the first month to establish the same rhythm.

narratives.prompt: Walk through how T/O changes and billet management actually work in practice.
narratives.response: On paper, the process is clean: a unit submits a T/O Change Request through their chain of command, it routes to M&RA, then to PP&O for OccField manager concurrence, then to the T/O Review Board. In practice, it is messier. About half of the TOCRs I see are incomplete -- missing justification, wrong T/O line numbers, or no endorsement from the intermediate command. I send these back immediately with a checklist of what is needed rather than trying to fix them myself. For the ones that are complete, I run my analysis in about a week: check current fill rates for the requested MOS and grade, look at the cumulative impact of all pending TOCRs for that MOS, and coordinate with the monitor on assignment feasibility. My concurrence or non-concurrence memo goes through the Branch Chief who usually approves within two days. The real bottleneck is the T/O Review Board, which meets quarterly. Miss the submission deadline by even one day and your TOCR waits until the next quarter. The informal reality is that about 30% of billet changes originate not from the formal TOCR process but from force structure reviews directed by higher headquarters, which bypass the normal timeline entirely.

narratives.prompt: How does school seat allocation and training pipeline coordination work for your field?
narratives.response: School seat allocation for the 06XX field runs through TECOM with significant input from this billet. Each fiscal year I submit projected requirements through the Training Input Plan, which is due in March. I base my numbers on current inventory, projected losses from EAS and lateral moves, and any pending billet changes that would increase or decrease demand. TECOM takes my input, cross-references it with schoolhouse capacity constraints -- primarily instructor availability and facility throughput -- and publishes the annual quota. The quotas are then allocated across the MEFs and supporting establishment by M&RA based on unit priority. In practice, the biggest issue is not the total number of seats but instructor staffing at MCCES. When instructor billets are gapped, class sizes shrink and the pipeline contracts regardless of demand. I monitor instructor fill rates monthly through the MCCES Director. The other recurring issue is course attrition. When attrition exceeds 15%, I coordinate with the schoolhouse to understand whether it is a screening issue, a curriculum problem, or a quality-of-instruction issue. Mid-year seat adjustments happen about twice a year and always require quick-turn coordination between me, TECOM, and M&RA.

narratives.prompt: What career path concerns exist within this occupational field that you've been working on?
narratives.response: Three career path issues are active. First, the 0671 merger created uncertainty about promotion timelines for the absorbed 0621 and 0623 Marines. I worked with M&RA to ensure that time-in-grade credit transfers cleanly and that promotion board precepts reflect the merger, but individual Marines are still confused and the community needs better communication. I am drafting a MARADMIN with the monitor to clarify the transition timeline. Second, there is a promotion bottleneck at the SSgt-to-GySgt level in 0699 because the field is overmanned at the senior grades relative to billets. I recommended a targeted SNCO lateral move incentive at the last symposium, which got mixed reception, but the data supports it. Third, career-enhancing billets are not evenly distributed across the force. Marines stationed at supporting establishment units have fewer opportunities for key billets compared to those at MEF headquarters or the schoolhouse. I am working on updating the MOS Roadmap to identify equivalent career-enhancing billets outside the operating forces so career planners and monitors can make better assignment recommendations for Marines at MCICOM and other supporting commands.

entries.title: MOS Manual Change Process
entries.content:
## Steps
1. Identify the requirement for change — sources include fleet feedback from OccField symposiums, TECOM identifying training pipeline misalignments, or your own MOS health data analysis showing structural problems in the OccField
2. Draft the proposed change using the MCO 1200.17 change template on PP&O SharePoint (OccField Manager Resources folder) — include specific MOS(s) affected, proposed changes to title/description/prerequisites/PMOS-to-AMOS relationships, justification narrative with specific numbers (Marines affected, billets changed, training pipeline delta), and impact assessment
3. Conduct informal pre-coordination with three key stakeholders before entering the formal staffing cycle: M&RA monitor (will this create assignment problems?), TECOM training POC (can the schoolhouse support this?), and Branch Chief (does this align with PP&O priorities?) — this step is not optional and skipping it is the number one reason changes get rejected later
4. Route through PP&O internal review — Branch Chief reviews and endorses, then it goes to DirOps for signature; typical turnaround is 1-2 weeks if you have pre-briefed DirOps, longer if you have not
5. Submit to M&RA (MPP-40) for formal coordination — they assess manning impact, assignment feasibility, and consistency with other pending changes; expect 2-4 weeks and be ready for pushback if the change creates unfillable billets
6. Simultaneously submit to TECOM for training pipeline impact review — they confirm they can adjust curriculum, instructor staffing, and throughput to support the change; expect 3-6 weeks for significant changes
7. For major changes (new MOS creation, deletion, significant prerequisite changes), route through CMC Staff for final approval — expect 4-8 weeks at this stage
8. Once all concurrences are received, submit approved change to Publications office for formatting and incorporation into the next MOS Manual update
9. Monitor publication and distribution via MCPEL — effective dates are typically 60-90 days after publication to allow units to adjust

## Key Contacts
- M&RA Monitor, MPP-40 — concurrence on manning impact and assignment feasibility; will non-concur if change creates unfillable billets
- TECOM Training Pipeline Manager — concurrence on training pipeline impacts, curriculum and instructor adjustments
- Branch Chief, POF — internal PP&O review and endorsement before routing to DirOps
- DirOps, PP&O — signature authority for minor/moderate changes; routes major changes to DC PP&O
- Publications Office — formatting and incorporation into MOS Manual; handles MCPEL distribution

## Timeline
Total process runs 17-24 weeks end to end. Initiation and drafting takes weeks 1-4. Formal staffing (M&RA, TECOM, and CMC Staff coordination) runs weeks 5-16. Publication and distribution takes weeks 17-24. The most common delays come from incomplete impact assessments, lack of fleet endorsements, and timing conflicts with POM cycles. Approval authority varies: DirOps for minor changes (title, description wording), DC PP&O for moderate changes (prerequisite adjustments), CMC for major changes (new MOS, deletion, restructure).

## Additional Notes
This is the single most complex and high-visibility process you own. A MOS Manual change affects every Marine in the OccField, every school seat allocation, and every T/O across the force. Always include at least two endorsements from operational commands (MEF or MEB level preferred) — lack of fleet input makes reviewers skeptical. Check the PP&O change tracker before initiating to avoid timing conflicts with other pending changes. Never skip the informal pre-coordination step; the formal process is slow enough that blindsiding M&RA or TECOM adds months to the timeline.

entries.title: Manning Request and Billet Change Procedures
entries.content:
## Steps
1. Receive the T/O Change Request (TOCR) — requests originate from unit commanding officers routing through MEF/supporting establishment G-1 to M&RA to your desk, from force structure reviews directed by higher HQ, or proactively from your own MOS health data analysis
2. Verify the TOCR is complete — check for justification narrative, specific billet details (MOS, grade, unit, T/O line number), and endorsements from the chain of command; send incomplete packages back immediately with a checklist of what is needed rather than trying to fix them yourself
3. Conduct OccField impact analysis — assess how the change affects total force inventory by answering three key questions: Does this create a billet we cannot fill? Does it shift grade structure in a way that hurts career progression? Does it duplicate a capability already on the T/O?
4. Coordinate with the M&RA monitor on assignment feasibility, TECOM on training pipeline impact if a new MOS is involved, and the requesting command's G-1 for any clarifications needed
5. Draft your concurrence or non-concurrence memo with specific data: current fill rates for the MOS and grade, projected inventory, cumulative impact of all pending TOCRs for that MOS, and any conditions on your concurrence
6. Route your recommendation through the Branch Chief (typically approves within two days) to DirOps for the PP&O position
7. Submit to the T/O Review Board for final adjudication — the board meets quarterly (Jan, Apr, Jul, Oct) with submission deadlines six weeks prior; miss the deadline by even one day and the TOCR waits until the next quarter

## Key Contacts
- M&RA Monitor, MPP-40 — assignment feasibility assessment and fill priority coordination
- TECOM Training Pipeline Manager — training pipeline impact assessment for any new MOS requirements
- Requesting Command G-1 — clarifications on billet justification and operational need
- Branch Chief, POF — reviews and approves your recommendation memo before routing to DirOps
- T/O Review Board — quarterly adjudication body for final approval

## Timeline
Simple billet swap (same MOS, different grade): 2-3 months. New billet addition with funded manpower: 4-6 months. Billet addition requiring POM funding: 12-18 months minimum (must make the next POM cycle). Unit activation or major restructure: 18-24 months. Approval authority scales with scope: 1-5 billets same unit goes to T/O Review Board, 6-20 billets or cross-unit requires DC PP&O approval, 20+ billets or new unit activation is a CMC-level decision.

## Additional Notes
About 30% of billet changes originate not from the formal TOCR process but from force structure reviews directed by higher headquarters, which bypass the normal timeline entirely. Never concur on a billet addition if the MOS is already below 90% fill — you are creating a hollow billet that makes your metrics worse. Always check grade structure: a request for a GySgt billet in an MOS where every GySgt slot is already filled means the billet will sit empty for years. Track cumulative impact of all pending TOCRs in aggregate — ten small requests can fundamentally reshape your OccField's grade structure without anyone noticing until it is too late.

entries.title: TECOM School Seat and Curriculum Coordination
entries.content:
## Steps
1. Pull current inventory data from TFDW, projected losses (EAS, lateral moves, retirement), and any pending billet changes that would increase or decrease demand for each MOS-producing course in the OccField
2. Submit projected training requirements to TECOM during the annual Training Input Plan (TIP) cycle, due in March for the following fiscal year — your numbers directly drive how many school seats TECOM allocates
3. TECOM establishes annual school seat quotas based on your input, historical throughput, and schoolhouse instructor and facility capacity; review the published quotas and flag any discrepancies immediately
4. Monitor training pipeline monthly through the Training Pipeline Sync with TECOM — review current course throughput, attrition rates, upcoming class start dates, and instructor staffing issues
5. Collect fleet feedback on graduate quality from operational commands through the OccField symposium, direct liaison with unit SNCOs, and battalion-level contacts — document specific examples of skill gaps or strengths
6. Present fleet feedback at the quarterly Curriculum Review Board and propose specific changes to learning objectives, course length, or practical application requirements
7. If throughput problems arise mid-year (instructor shortages, facility issues, attrition spikes), coordinate immediately with M&RA to adjust assignment projections and with TECOM on mitigation options
8. For curriculum changes, track implementation through TECOM — minor changes (updated references, adjusted exercises) can happen at the schoolhouse level; major changes (course length, new POI) require CG TECOM approval and take 6-12 months

## Key Contacts
- TECOM Training Pipeline Manager, Quantico — primary POC for all school seat issues; schedule quarterly face-to-face visits
- Schoolhouse Director/OIC, MCCES — direct liaison for day-to-day training issues; maintain a monthly phone call minimum
- M&RA Monitor, MPP-40 — coordinate assignment projection adjustments when quotas change mid-year
- MEF G-3 Communications Planners — provide fleet feedback on graduate quality and emerging skill requirements

## Timeline
Annual TIP submission due in March for the following fiscal year. Monthly Training Pipeline Sync call with TECOM. Quarterly Curriculum Review Board (in-person at Quantico preferred). Annual face-to-face planning conference at Quantico during the TIP development period (January-March). Mid-year seat adjustments happen about twice a year and always require quick-turn coordination between you, TECOM, and M&RA.

## Additional Notes
The number one limiter on school seat capacity is instructor billets — if instructor billets are unfilled, class sizes shrink regardless of student demand. Monitor instructor fill rates as closely as student throughput. If course attrition spikes above 15%, investigate immediately (screening criteria, instructor quality, or curriculum mismatch); do not wait for the quarterly review. You do not directly assign seats to units, but you heavily influence the allocation formula through your requirement inputs. Access TECMIS (Training and Education Command Information System) for the system of record on school seat data — request read access through TECOM G-6, expect 3-4 weeks for the access request.

entries.title: OccField Specialist Symposium / Conference Planning
entries.content:
## Steps
1. At T-6 months, determine dates and venue — the National Conference Center at Quantico is the default, but if attendance exceeds 80 you may need the Clubs or an off-base venue; reserve immediately as they book up fast, especially in spring
2. Draft the initial concept paper including purpose, scope, proposed attendees (by billet, not name), draft agenda topics, and estimated cost; route through Branch Chief for approval
3. Submit a funding request through PP&O comptroller — typical costs run $15K-$25K for a 3-day symposium (room rental, A/V, materials, per diem for non-local attendees); travel costs are borne by sending commands
4. At T-4 months, finalize the attendee list and send invitations on official PP&O letterhead including dates, location, agenda overview, travel/billeting guidance, and uniform requirements
5. Identify and invite guest speakers — DC PP&O or DirOps for opening remarks, a TECOM representative and M&RA monitor for presentations, and fleet SNCOs as panel members for credibility
6. Develop working group agendas — each working group should address a specific OccField issue (training pipeline, retention, career progression, emerging requirements) with a defined deliverable
7. At T-2 months, collect RSVPs and resolve travel issues (have Branch Chief engage reluctant commands), finalize agenda, distribute read-ahead materials (current MOS health data, pending policy changes, specific questions), and coordinate A/V, printing, and admin support
8. At T-1 week, confirm all logistics (room setup, A/V test, printed materials, name tents, sign-in roster), pre-brief guest speakers on time allocation and themes, and assign note-takers for each session
9. Execute the 3-day symposium: Day 1 for opening remarks and MOS health overview, Day 2 for working groups, Day 3 for outbriefs, consensus building, and action item assignment
10. Publish the after-action report within 30 days including all working group recommendations, action items with owners and due dates, and key takeaways; brief DirOps on outcomes

## Key Contacts
- Branch Chief, POF — approves the concept paper and can engage reluctant commands to send attendees
- PP&O Comptroller — processes the funding request for the symposium budget
- DirOps, PP&O — opening remarks speaker and primary audience for post-symposium recommendations
- TECOM Training Pipeline Manager — guest presenter on training pipeline updates
- M&RA Monitor, MPP-40 — guest presenter on the assignment landscape
- National Conference Center, Quantico — venue reservation and logistics coordination

## Timeline
Start planning at T-6 months (November for a May symposium). Concept paper and venue reservation by T-6. Invitations out by T-4 months. RSVPs collected and agenda finalized by T-2 months. Final logistics confirmed at T-1 week. Execution is typically 3 days (Tuesday-Thursday). After-action report published within 30 days of completion.

## Additional Notes
Keep working groups small (8-12 people) — larger groups devolve into unfocused discussion. Assign a facilitator to each group who is not the OccField manager; you should float between groups to ensure productive discussion. Record everything — symposium recommendations become the justification backbone for MOS Manual changes and POM submissions. Track every action item to completion after the symposium; if recommendations die on the vine, attendees will not take the next symposium seriously. This is your single best opportunity to gather fleet feedback and build consensus on OccField direction.

entries.title: Quarterly MOS Health Reporting
entries.content:
## Steps
1. At T-21 days, pull data from all five sources: TFDW for inventory by MOS and grade (caveat: lags 60-90 days), MCTFS for near-real-time individual Marine status, M&RA monitor's live tracking spreadsheet for current fill rates and projected gains/losses (your most accurate source — get a copy weekly), TECOM training pipeline reports for throughput and attrition, and MMEA retention data by MOS
2. Begin building the slide deck using the standard PP&O MOS Health template — the format is 5 slides maximum: Executive Summary (Green/Amber/Red by MOS, top 3 issues, recommended actions), Inventory vs. Requirement table, Pipeline Status, Retention Analysis (compare to Service-wide averages, flag any MOS below 80% of target), and Issues and Actions
3. At T-14 days, complete the analysis — identify key trends (what is improving, declining, stable), draft recommendations for each finding, and prepare backup slides with detailed data for anticipated DirOps questions
4. At T-7 days, pre-brief the Branch Chief — this is not optional; the Branch Chief will catch issues you missed and help refine the narrative; incorporate their feedback and finalize slides
5. On brief day, present to DirOps — the format is 15 minutes for presentation plus 10 minutes for Q&A; lead with the bottom line, tell a story rather than just showing numbers, and always have a recommendation for every problem you present
6. After the brief, distribute the report to the M&RA monitor (their data feeds the report and they need to see your analysis), TECOM training POC (pipeline-related findings), and the OccField community via official distribution list (sanitized version without internal recommendations)

## Key Contacts
- M&RA Monitor, MPP-40 — provides the most accurate current manning data via their live tracking spreadsheet; primary data source and distribution recipient
- TECOM Training Pipeline Manager — provides monthly throughput, attrition, and graduation projection reports; receives pipeline-related findings
- MMEA — provides quarterly reenlistment and retention data by MOS
- Branch Chief, POF — mandatory pre-brief at T-7 days; refines narrative and catches analytical gaps
- DirOps, PP&O — the primary audience; expects brevity, recommendations, and forward-looking analysis

## Timeline
The quarterly cycle runs on a 21-day preparation timeline. Data pull and deck building in week 1 (T-21 to T-14). Analysis completion and recommendation drafting in week 2 (T-14 to T-7). Branch Chief pre-brief and slide finalization in week 3 (T-7 to brief day). Briefs occur in January (Q1, covering Oct-Dec), April (Q2, covering Jan-Mar), July (Q3, covering Apr-Jun), and October (Q4, covering Jul-Sep and full FY rollup).

## Additional Notes
Never brief TFDW numbers alone as current inventory — always cross-reference with the monitor's tracker and qualify your data source and date. Track trends, not just snapshots: a 92% fill rate that was 97% last quarter is more alarming than an 85% fill rate that was 80% last quarter. DirOps sees dozens of data briefs; make yours memorable by connecting data to operational impact. The Q4 brief is the most important because it covers the full fiscal year and sets the context for the next year's priorities. After any DirOps interaction, send a summary of action items and decisions to the Branch Chief within 2 hours.

entries.title: Career Path Development and PME Coordination
entries.content:
## Steps
1. Define the MOS progression from entry-level PMOS through advanced MOSs and any AMOS qualifications — document which lateral moves are encouraged, which are discouraged, and why; this becomes the backbone of career counseling for the entire OccField
2. Identify career-enhancing billets at each grade — not all billets are equal, and documenting which positions carry more weight for promotion boards helps Marines and monitors make informed assignment decisions
3. Map Professional Military Education requirements to career milestones — Sergeants need the Sergeants Course before the next promotion board, SNCOs need the Advanced Course, officers need equivalent PME gating; identify any OccField-specific advanced courses
4. Identify civilian certifications, joint qualifications, and additional military courses that enhance career progression — note which are mandatory versus recommended and update this list annually
5. Conduct quarterly sync with Marine Corps University (MCU) / Education Command on PME seat allocation for your OccField — PME seats are competitive and limited, so advocate early for your Marines
6. Provide annual input to the Commandant's PME guidance on any OccField-specific requirements and coordinate on MOS-specific advanced course needs
7. Update the MOS Roadmap annually during the MOS Manual review cycle — the roadmap is published on MCCLL and distributed through the OccField community; this is the primary career path reference document
8. Monitor grade distribution quarterly to catch pyramiding problems early, track lateral move rates to detect career path attractiveness issues, and review PME completion rates to identify whether gaps are seat allocation or command priority problems

## Key Contacts
- M&RA Monitor, MPP-40 — coordinates assignment decisions that directly affect career progression; key partner for ensuring career-enhancing billets are prioritized in the assignment plan
- MCU/Education Command — PME seat allocation, Commandant's PME guidance input, advanced course coordination
- TECOM Training Pipeline Manager — coordinates MOS-specific advanced training courses and certifications
- OccField Community via symposium — primary venue for gathering fleet feedback on career path satisfaction and identifying needed changes

## Timeline
Quarterly sync with MCU/Education Command on PME seats. Annual MOS Roadmap update during the MOS Manual review cycle (October-February). Annual input to Commandant's PME guidance. Career path review should be a standing agenda item at the annual OccField symposium. Monitor grade distribution and lateral move rates quarterly as part of the MOS Health Report.

## Additional Notes
You do not directly control assignments or promotions, but you shape the environment: which MOSs exist, what the prerequisites are, which billets are on the T/O, and how the career path is communicated. Grade pyramiding is the most common structural problem — if too many senior billets exist relative to junior billets, Marines cannot promote because there are no billets above them to move into. If Marines are laterally moving out of your OccField at high rates, the career path may be perceived as unattractive; survey departing Marines to understand why. Career-enhancing billets are not evenly distributed across the force — Marines at supporting establishment units have fewer opportunities compared to those at MEF headquarters or the schoolhouse. Work to identify equivalent career-enhancing billets outside the operating forces.

entries.title: Interservice Coordination for Joint Billets
entries.content:
## Steps
1. Maintain a running inventory of all joint billets requiring Marines from your OccField — sources include the Joint Manning Document (JMD) for combatant commands (CENTCOM, INDOPACOM, EUCOM), defense agency billets (DIA, NSA, CYBERCOM), NATO and coalition billets, and Joint Staff billets at the Pentagon; track location, grade, MOS, fill status, rotation date, and owning organization POC for each
2. Participate in the annual joint billet review coordinated by the Joint Manpower Program office — provide input on which billets your OccField can fill, which are unfillable and why, and any proposed changes to the JMD
3. When joint billet fills are coordinated through M&RA (MMEA for enlisted, MMOA for officers), concur on the MOS appropriateness of the fill — your role is MOS validation, not specific Marine selection
4. Ensure career path documentation reflects JPME requirements — Marines filling joint billets at O-4 and above typically need Joint Professional Military Education Phase I completion
5. Coordinate with sister services annually: Army Signal Branch OccField Manager at HRC (Fort Knox) at the Joint Communications Support Forum in September, Navy Information Warfare Community on SIGINT/EW-related billets, and Air Force Cyber Operations on joint cyber billets and training standardization
6. For interservice transfers (governed by DoD Directive 1300.4), provide concurrence on MOS equivalency for inbound transfers and retention impact assessments for outbound transfers
7. Coordinate with TECOM on reintegration training for Marines returning from joint assignments who may need refresher training on USMC-specific systems and procedures

## Key Contacts
- M&RA (MMEA/MMOA) — coordinates joint billet fill actions; enlisted through MMEA, officers through MMOA
- Joint Manpower Program Office — runs the annual joint billet review
- Army Signal Branch OccField Manager, HRC Fort Knox — primary interservice counterpart; annual coordination at the Joint Communications Support Forum in September
- Navy Information Warfare Community — SIGINT and EW-related joint billet coordination
- Air Force Cyber Operations — joint cyber billet and training standardization coordination

## Timeline
Annual joint billet review coordinated by the Joint Manpower Program office (timing varies, typically mid-fiscal year). Joint Communications Support Forum with Army Signal Branch held annually in September. Interservice transfer actions are ad hoc. Joint billet rotation dates vary by billet and should be tracked individually on your running inventory.

## Additional Notes
Joint billets consume your best talent — they typically require experienced SNCOs and mid-grade officers, so account for this in your manning projections. Never let joint billets go unfilled; unfilled joint billets generate negative attention at the SECDEF level. If you cannot fill a billet, formally request it be re-coded to a different MOS or deleted from the JMD rather than leaving it vacant. The Army operates at a fundamentally different scale (tens of thousands of soldiers versus your hundreds or low thousands of Marines), so be patient with their coordination tempo. Marines returning from joint assignments bring valuable experience but the transition back requires attention.

entries.title: FY25 MOS Restructure: Merge 0621/0623 into 0671
entries.content:
## The Decision
The Marine Corps will consolidate MOS 0621 (Field Radio Operator) and MOS 0623 (MAGTF Planner, Communications) into an expanded MOS 0671 (Data Systems Administrator), effective 1 October 2026 with a 24-month transition window for existing Marines in the legacy MOSs. Implementation runs in three phases: Phase 1 (Oct 2025 - Mar 2026) has TECOM developing the unified curriculum with expanded radio and planning modules; Phase 2 (Apr - Sep 2026) graduates the last legacy classes and begins the unified course; Phase 3 (Oct 2026 - Sep 2028) is the transition window where existing 0621/0623 Marines reclassify to 0671 via MOS Administrative Action with no course required for Marines with 2+ years in the legacy MOS. This decision is not readily reversible — once legacy MOSs are deleted and the training pipeline is consolidated, re-separating them would require a new MOS creation process taking 12-18 months minimum.

## Alternatives Considered
Status quo was rejected because inventory imbalances (0621 at 112% fill at E-4/E-5 while 0671 sat at 78%) and training inefficiencies (three separate courses with overlapping content consuming instructor billets) would persist indefinitely. Merging 0621 into 0623 only was rejected because 0623 was too narrow a platform to absorb 0621 skills — the combined MOS needed to be broader. Creating a new MOS (0625) was rejected because creating an MOS from scratch introduces more administrative burden than expanding an existing one, and 0671 already had the closest training pipeline to support the merger. A phased approach (merge 0621/0623 first, evaluate, then consider 0671 later) was rejected as adding unnecessary delay when the analysis clearly supported a single consolidated MOS.

## Rationale
Four factors drove this decision. First, converging skill sets: modern communications systems increasingly require both traditional radio operation and data networking skills, and the distinction between a radio operator and a data systems administrator has become artificial at the tactical level. Second, inventory pressure: the chronic overstaffing of 0621 at E-4/E-5 (112% fill) alongside persistent understaffing of 0671 (78% fill) meant the combined inventory would better match total requirements under a single MOS. Third, training efficiency: maintaining three separate courses with overlapping content consumed instructor billets that could be consolidated — TECOM estimated a savings of 8 instructor billets. Fourth, fleet feedback: the FY24 OccField symposium produced a unanimous recommendation from the field-grade working group to consolidate, and operational commanders reported no meaningful distinction in how they employed 0621s and 0623s.

## Impact
Training pipeline sees a net reduction of 3 weeks in combined training time and a savings of 8 instructor billets that are being reassigned to support the expanded 0671 course. Manning is projected to improve from 78% combined fill to 94% within 18 months of implementation. Career progression benefits from a healthier grade pyramid with more opportunities at the E-6/E-7 level under the combined MOS. The early attrition data from the bridge course is running higher than projected (18% versus the 10% planned), which needs continued monitoring. The post-merger health of 0671 after absorbing 0621 and 0623 Marines is one of the top OccField health concerns to track through the transition period.

meta.alternatives: Status quo (rejected: persistent imbalances); Merge 0621 into 0623 only (rejected: too narrow); Create new MOS 0625 (rejected: unnecessary admin burden); Phased approach (rejected: unnecessary delay)
meta.outcome: Merger approved by CMC staff. Implementation in three phases through Sep 2028. Training pipeline consolidation underway.

entries.title: Training Pipeline Adjustment: Reduced Course Length for 0612
entries.content:
## The Decision
The formal MOS-producing course for 0612 (Tactical Switching Operator) was reduced from 12 weeks to 9 weeks, effective with Class 26-004 starting March 2026. Three weeks of legacy AN/TTC-56 switching platform instruction were removed and replaced with a condensed module focused on the modernized IP-based replacement system. Three instructor billets freed by the course reduction are being reassigned to the 0671 merged course. This decision will be formally reviewed at the Q4 FY26 Curriculum Review Board (July 2026) based on the first two graduating classes under the new POI. Success metrics: course pass rate remains above 90%, unit commander feedback on graduate readiness stays at satisfactory or above, and annual graduate count increases by 35-45 Marines.

## Alternatives Considered
Maintaining the 12-week course was rejected because 3 weeks of the curriculum were dedicated to the legacy AN/TTC-56 system that is being replaced across all operating forces — the schoolhouse OIC presented survey data showing near-zero relevance of that training to fleet duties. Removing the 3 weeks and replacing them with cyber content was rejected due to scope creep and insufficient instructor qualification for the proposed cyber modules. Keeping the course length and reallocating the 3 weeks to other topics was considered but rejected because the primary need was throughput improvement to address the 23% school seat shortage, not additional curriculum content.

## Rationale
Three factors drove this decision. First, equipment fielding: the legacy AN/TTC-56 is being replaced by the modernized IP-based system across all operating forces, and spending 3 weeks training Marines on equipment they will never see in the fleet was wasteful — the fielding plan shows less than 5% of units still on legacy systems by mid-2026. Second, pipeline throughput: the 0612 MOS had a critical school seat shortage (30% under quota this FY), and reducing course length by 3 weeks allows an additional class cycle per fiscal year, increasing annual throughput by approximately 40 graduates. Third, TECOM's own recommendation based on fleet survey data confirmed the removed content had near-zero operational relevance. Risk of graduates arriving less technically proficient is mitigated by the fact that removed content was legacy-specific and the replacement system is operationally simpler. Fleet feedback will be monitored for 12 months post-implementation.

## Impact
The shortened course allows an additional class cycle per fiscal year, projecting an increase of 35-45 annual graduates that directly addresses the 0612 school seat shortage. The 3 instructor billets freed by the reduction are being reassigned to the 0671 merged course, supporting that transition. The few units still operating legacy equipment (less than 5% by mid-2026) can provide on-the-job training. This decision is reversible if fleet feedback indicates graduate quality has degraded, though reverting would reduce throughput back to pre-change levels and re-create the pipeline bottleneck.

meta.alternatives: Maintain 12-week course (rejected: wasted training time); Remove 3 weeks and add cyber content (rejected: scope creep, insufficient instructor qualification)
meta.outcome: Course reduced to 9 weeks. First class under new POI starts March 2026.

entries.title: School Seat Allocation: Priority to I MEF over II MEF
entries.content:
## The Decision
For FY26 Q2-Q4 school seat allocation for MOS 0612 and 0631 courses, I MEF receives 60% of available seats and II MEF receives 40%, departing from the standard 50/50 split. This temporary reallocation applies for three quarters only (January through September 2026), with the standard allocation formula resuming in FY27 Q1. Conditions: if II MEF's deployment timeline accelerates, the allocation will be reassessed immediately; quarterly review at each MOS Health brief confirms the reallocation is achieving desired fill rates; and II MEF receives priority consideration if additional seats become available mid-year. All stakeholders concurred: I MEF G-1 provided specific unit fill data, II MEF G-1 accepted with the reversion guarantee, TECOM confirmed seat availability supports the reallocation without impacting total throughput, and the M&RA monitor adjusted assignment projections accordingly.

## Alternatives Considered
Maintaining the standard 50/50 split was rejected because I MEF's deploying battalions were at 72% fill for 0612 and 68% for 0631, well below the 80% deployment threshold — continuing 50/50 would have guaranteed deploying units shipped with critical communication billets unfilled. Requesting additional seats from TECOM was rejected because there was no instructor capacity to support additional class sections. Shifting seats from III MEF was rejected due to Pacific posture requirements that made reducing III MEF allocations unacceptable. A 55/45 split was considered but rejected because the modeling showed it was insufficient to close the fill gap before the August 2026 deployment rotation.

## Rationale
The primary driver was deployment cycle alignment. I MEF has a major deployment rotation beginning August 2026, and their deploying battalions were at 72% fill for 0612 and 68% for 0631 — deploying below 80% fill creates unacceptable risk to mission. II MEF units recently returned from deployment and are in a reconstitution phase where their fill rates are lower overall but do not face the same deployment timeline pressure. The M&RA monitor confirmed the reallocation aligns with the assignment plan and will not create downstream problems for II MEF's next deployment cycle (projected FY27 Q2). The key to making this work was presenting clear deployment timeline data to all parties and guaranteeing the reversion to standard allocation — MEF G-1s have long memories and perceiving favoritism would damage the OccField manager's credibility with both MEFs.

## Impact
I MEF deploying battalions are projected to reach 80%+ fill for both 0612 and 0631 by the August 2026 deployment date. II MEF accepted a temporary reduction in school seat allocation in exchange for guaranteed reversion and priority consideration for mid-year additional seats. This decision required extensive pre-coordination across four stakeholder organizations and set a precedent for how deployment-driven seat reallocation requests should be handled — with data, transparency, and guaranteed reversion timelines. The approach should be documented as a template for future deployment cycle-driven allocation adjustments.

meta.alternatives: Maintain 50/50 split (rejected: I MEF deploys below threshold); Request additional seats from TECOM (rejected: no instructor capacity); Shift seats from III MEF (rejected: Pacific posture requirements)
meta.outcome: I MEF 60/40 priority allocation for 3 quarters. II MEF accepted with reversion guarantee.

entries.title: Monitor Coordination: Shift from Quarterly to Monthly Syncs
entries.content:
## The Decision
The standing coordination meeting between the OccField Manager and the M&RA monitor (MPP-40) shifted from quarterly to monthly, effective September 2025. The meeting is a 60-minute video call on the second Tuesday of each month, 1000-1100 EST. The standing agenda covers: current inventory snapshot, pending PCS actions, school seat pipeline status, retention data review, and open issues. Both parties maintain a shared running action log in a OneNote notebook in the PP&O Teams channel. Do not revert to quarterly — the monthly cadence is essential. If your monitor suggests quarterly is sufficient, politely decline. The investment of 12 hours per year in monthly syncs saves hundreds of hours in problem remediation.

## Alternatives Considered
Maintaining the quarterly cadence was rejected because it proved too slow to catch and resolve problems before they compounded — three specific failures were identified during the FY25 Q3 MOS Health brief review. Weekly calls were considered but rejected as excessive given current workload and the monitor's other OccField responsibilities. Bi-weekly calls were an acceptable option but monthly was determined to be sufficient based on the typical issue development timeline and the fact that ad hoc coordination still happens 2-3 times per week for specific assignment questions.

## Rationale
During the FY25 Q3 MOS Health brief, DirOps flagged that several manning issues had festered for months without resolution. Investigation revealed three specific failures caused by the quarterly cadence. First, a PCS misalignment sent 12 Marines to units without matching billets — by the time it was discovered at the quarterly meeting, the Marines were already in transit, costing 3 months of lost productivity and significant unit frustration. Second, a retention shortfall in MOS 0631 went unaddressed for 4 months because the data was not reviewed until the next quarterly sync, and by then the window for retention bonuses had closed for FY25. Third, school seat cancellations at TECOM were not communicated to the monitor for 6 weeks, causing downstream assignment mismatches. Each of these problems would have been caught and resolved within days under a monthly coordination rhythm.

## Impact
Since shifting to monthly coordination, PCS misalignment incidents dropped from 3-4 per quarter to zero. Open issues average resolution time decreased from 47 days to 12 days. The monitor relationship has significantly strengthened — the monitor now proactively flags issues before they appear in data, which is the strongest indicator that the cadence is working. The shared OneNote action log has become the single source of truth for all OccField manning coordination, replacing the informal email threads that previously made it difficult to track commitments. This change cost nothing except 12 hours per year of meeting time and produced measurable, significant improvement across every coordination metric.

meta.alternatives: Maintain quarterly cadence (rejected: too slow to catch issues); Weekly calls (considered but rejected as excessive given current workload); Bi-weekly calls (acceptable but monthly was sufficient)
meta.outcome: Monthly syncs implemented. Measurable improvement in issue resolution time and PCS accuracy.

entries.title: M&RA Monitor, MPP-40
entries.content: This is the single most important external relationship you maintain. The monitor at MPP-40 controls PCS orders for every Marine in your OccField, manages individual assignments, generates PCS orders, sets fill priorities when demand exceeds supply, and maintains the most current inventory data updated daily. Monthly sync is the second Tuesday at 1000-1100 EST covering inventory, pending PCS, school seat pipeline, retention, and open issues. Expect 2-3 ad hoc calls or emails per week on specific assignment questions — respond within 24 hours. During transfer season (Apr-Jun), coordination intensity doubles to near-daily contact. Build this relationship by bringing data instead of opinions, understanding their constraints (your OccField is one of many they manage), never escalating to M&RA leadership without first trying to resolve directly, providing early warning on retention problems or T/O changes, and visiting Arlington at least once per quarter. They need accurate T/O data, early notice of MOS Manual changes, concurrence on fill priorities within 48 hours, and feedback on how assignments are working at the fleet level.

meta.relationship: Primary coordination for all 06XX Manning. Call this person first for any assignment or inventory question. They control PCS orders and have final say on fill priorities. Build a strong working relationship — they can make or break your manning posture.

entries.title: TECOM Training Pipeline POC
entries.content: Your TECOM counterpart manages the supply side of your OccField equation — school seat allocation, curriculum content (POI, learning objectives, assessment standards), instructor staffing oversight, attrition tracking, and coordination with schoolhouse OICs on day-to-day training execution. When the training pipeline is healthy, manning problems are manageable; when it chokes, everything downstream breaks within 6-12 months. Coordination rhythm is monthly (Training Pipeline Sync via phone/VTC reviewing throughput, attrition, class schedules, emerging issues), quarterly (Curriculum Review Board in-person at Quantico with fleet feedback), and annually (Training Input Plan submission Jan-Mar). Visit the schoolhouse at least once per quarter — walk the training areas, talk to instructors, observe practical exercises. Instructor staffing is their biggest headache, so support their instructor fill requests through your M&RA coordination. Fleet feedback is your currency with TECOM: bring specific, constructive, documented feedback from operational commands about graduate quality. Respect the POI change process — do not ask for quick changes that undermine quality assurance. They need accurate training requirement projections, fleet feedback on graduate quality, support for instructor staffing requests, early warning on MOS Manual changes, and your attendance at Curriculum Review Boards.

meta.relationship: Manages school seat allocation, curriculum reviews, and instructor staffing. Contact when course throughput drops or curriculum needs updating. Schedule face-to-face at least once per quarter.

entries.title: DC I OccField Analyst
entries.content: The DC I OccField Analyst provides the intelligence community perspective on communications and data systems requirements, handling cross-OccField requirements analysis where 06XX and 26XX capabilities overlap, SIGINT/EW billet requirements that touch both fields, emerging technology assessments, and joint billet coordination for positions at NSA, CYBERCOM, or DIA requiring 06XX MOSs. Monthly coordination is a standing phone call on the third Thursday covering cross-OccField issues, pending billet changes, and upcoming policy changes, with 1-2 ad hoc coordination requests per month when DC I is staffing something that touches your equities. Engage this contact for any MOS Manual change involving SIGINT, EW, or cyber components, joint billet reviews, training pipeline changes affecting cross-OccField courses, and Force Design initiatives that merge or realign capabilities between 06XX and 26XX. The intelligence community operates with different classification constraints — if they are vague about a request's rationale, it may be because they cannot share the full picture at the UNCLASS level. Be responsive and transparent with your manning data; it builds trust and ensures their requirements are grounded in reality.

meta.relationship: Provides intelligence community perspective on 06XX requirements. Coordinates SIGINT/EW billet requirements. Key contact for any 26XX-related cross-OccField issues.

entries.title: Sister Service Liaison (Army Signal)
entries.content: The Army Signal Branch equivalent of your billet sits at HRC in Fort Knox, Kentucky. This is a less frequent coordination touchpoint but important for joint billet management (several combatant command billets can be filled by either Army Signal or Marine 06XX Marines), training interoperability (the Army Signal School at Fort Eisenhower occasionally offers seats to Marine students for specialized courses), doctrine and TTPs (the Army publishes ATP 6-02 series signal doctrine that influences joint operations), and equipment commonality as both services field convergent networking capabilities like the Integrated Tactical Network. Coordination rhythm is quarterly phone calls to review joint billet status and interoperability issues, with the annual Joint Communications Support Forum in September as the primary face-to-face venue — submit your travel request early as it is always oversubscribed. The Army operates at a fundamentally different scale (tens of thousands of soldiers versus your hundreds or low thousands of Marines), so be patient with their coordination tempo. The relationship is valuable because the Army has already solved many problems you will encounter; their experience with MOS restructuring, training pipeline management at scale, and retention programs can inform your approach.

meta.relationship: Coordinates on joint billet management, interoperability requirements, and cross-service training opportunities. Annual joint conference in September.

entries.title: POF Branch Chief
entries.content: Your direct supervisor and the gatekeeper for everything that leaves the branch. Daily standup at 0800 (15 minutes, 60-second updates per person on yesterday's actions, today's priorities, and issues needing attention) plus an end-of-day check-in via email or Teams to flag anything that changed. The Branch Chief expects no surprises (inform them immediately if something is going sideways — they would rather hear bad news from you first than from DirOps), complete staff work (bring a recommended solution with every problem), accurate data (double-check numbers before briefing), and met deadlines (request extensions before the due date, never miss silently). Approval authority covers routine correspondence, travel requests, conference/symposium concept papers, draft MOS Manual change packages before DirOps routing, and data call responses. Not the approval authority for MOS Manual changes (DirOps or higher), T/O changes above minor threshold, or budget requests. Communication preferences: face-to-face or phone for complex issues, email for documentation, Teams for quick questions, and hates long emails — keep written communication under 150 words with attachments for details. A senior O-5 or junior O-6 who has limited patience for excuses; build trust through consistently delivering quality work on time and you will receive significantly more autonomy and top cover.

meta.relationship: Direct supervisor. Daily standup at 0800. Approves all OccField manager actions before routing to DirOps. Keep informed of any manning shortfalls or MOS health issues immediately — no surprises.

entries.title: DirOps, PP&O
entries.content: A one-star general officer (BGen) or senior colonel who oversees all operational planning functions within PP&O. You do not interact with DirOps daily, but when you do the stakes are high. Interaction model: weekly status brief (your input feeds through the Branch Chief, you may be pulled in for OccField-specific questions), quarterly MOS Health brief (you present directly — your highest-visibility recurring event), and decision briefs for major OccField decisions prepared through the Branch Chief. Critical rule: never go directly to DirOps unless it is an emergency; all routine communication flows through the Branch Chief. DirOps values brevity (lead with the bottom line), recommendations (never present a problem without a proposed solution), intellectual honesty (zero tolerance for cherry-picked statistics), and forward-looking analysis (thinks in 2-5 year horizons, not tactical minutiae). Decision authority includes approving MOS Manual change packages before DC PP&O routing, T/O changes above branch-chief threshold, directing studies on OccField health, and emergency manning actions. Always rehearse with the Branch Chief first, prepare backup slides with detailed data, be ready for comparison questions across OccFields, and send a summary of action items to the Branch Chief within 2 hours of any DirOps interaction.

meta.relationship: Reviews weekly status brief. Approves major decisions (MOS restructures, T/O changes over threshold). Brief through Branch Chief first — never go direct unless emergency.

entries.title: Annual MOS Manual Review Cycle
entries.content: The cornerstone annual event for every OccField manager — your opportunity to make structural changes to MOSs that shape force management for years. The cycle runs October (data call and kickoff, pull MOS health data, review symposium recommendations, draft proposed changes) through December (submit drafts to Branch Chief with justification, manning impact, and training pipeline analysis) through February (formal staffing with M&RA, TECOM, and CMC Staff review — this is where most changes die, so pre-coordination in October/November dramatically improves survival rate) through April (publication of updated manual). Start preparation in September, not October: pull TFDW inventory data, request retention data from M&RA, review TECOM pipeline throughput, compile fleet feedback, identify and prioritize candidate changes, pre-coordinate with the monitor and TECOM POC, and brief the Branch Chief on planned submissions. Missing the cycle means waiting another full year.

entries.title: POM Budget Submission
entries.content: The only mechanism to get new funding for your OccField's training and manning requirements — miss the POM window and you wait two years. The POM translates strategy into funded programs for a 5-year period (Future Years Defense Program). Timeline runs November (requirements development — review unfunded needs, prioritize by operational impact, coordinate with TECOM and M&RA) through December-January (write Program Change Proposals with description, justification, cost estimate by FY, risk if not funded, and Force Design alignment — the narrative is everything, tie every dollar to operational capability) through February (submit finalized PCPs through Branch Chief to PP&O POM coordinator, DirOps approves) through March-May (Service-level defense and adjudication, be ready to defend in POM Issue Papers) through June-July (POM published, review for your requirements, document unfunded items as UFRs for possible Congressional adds). Common killers: late submissions (dead on arrival, no exceptions), vague cost estimates (provide detailed line-item breakdowns), and uncoordinated TECOM requirements (gets flagged and deprioritized).

entries.title: Quarterly MOS Health Brief to DirOps
entries.content: Your highest-visibility recurring deliverable — four times a year you stand in front of a general officer and account for OccField health. Schedule: Q1 in January (Oct-Dec data), Q2 in April (Jan-Mar), Q3 in July (Apr-Jun), Q4 in October (Jul-Sep and full FY rollup). Preparation runs 21 days: week 1 pull data from TFDW, MCTFS, M&RA monitor spreadsheet, MMEA retention data, and TECOM throughput; week 2 complete analysis, identify trends, draft executive summary and recommendations, prepare backup slides, and pre-brief the Branch Chief (mandatory); week 3 incorporate feedback, finalize slides, distribute pre-read to DirOps exec 48 hours prior, and rehearse within the 15-minute presentation plus 10-minute Q&A window. Format is 5 slides maximum: Executive Summary (Green/Amber/Red by MOS, top 3 findings, recommendations), Inventory Dashboard, Pipeline Status, Retention, and Issues and Actions. Post-brief: send action item summary to Branch Chief within 2 hours, update the issues tracker, distribute sanitized version to M&RA monitor and TECOM POC within 1 week. Anticipate recurring DirOps questions about Service-average comparisons, biggest risk for next quarter, top unfunded need, and retention bonus effectiveness.

entries.title: OccField Specialist Symposium
entries.content: Held annually in May, this is the single most effective mechanism for gathering fleet feedback, building OccField consensus, and generating justification data for MOS Manual changes and POM submissions. Planning milestones: November (venue reservation and concept paper to Branch Chief), December (approval and budget coordination with PP&O comptroller, typical cost $15K-$25K), January (finalize attendee list and send invitations), February (RSVPs, agenda development, guest speakers), March (finalize agenda and distribute read-ahead materials), April (final logistics), May (execute 3-day symposium), June (publish after-action report and brief DirOps). Three-day format: Day 1 is state of the OccField with opening remarks and MOS health overview plus M&RA and TECOM presentations; Day 2 breaks into 4-5 working groups each producing recommendations with supporting data; Day 3 covers outbriefs, consensus building, and action item assignment. Travel costs are borne by sending commands — include this in invitations. The after-action report becomes the justification backbone for the next MOS Manual review cycle and POM submissions, so track every action item to completion.

entries.title: TECOM Curriculum Review Board
entries.content: The formal mechanism for reviewing and updating course content for all MOS-producing courses in your OccField — missing a CRB means waiting another quarter to address training quality issues. Held quarterly at TECOM headquarters in Quantico: Q1 in Dec/Jan, Q2 in Mar/Apr, Q3 in Jun/Jul, Q4 in Sep/Oct. Preparation starts at T-14 days: collect fleet feedback from at least 3 operational units on graduate performance, review course attrition data (investigate anything above 15%), check status on previous CRB action items, and draft any curriculum change proposals in TECOM format (current state, proposed change, justification, resource impact). Attendees include OccField Manager, TECOM Training Pipeline Manager, schoolhouse OIC and Chief Instructor, and sometimes M&RA. Your role is to present the fleet perspective, advocate for changes aligning training with operational requirements, and provide manning context for course capacity decisions. Decision authority: minor changes (references, lesson plans) implemented by schoolhouse OIC immediately; moderate changes (course length up to 1 week, new learning objectives) require Pipeline Manager approval; major changes (course length over 1 week, new course creation) require CG TECOM approval and take 6-12 months. Post-CRB: document decisions within 72 hours, distribute summary to Branch Chief and M&RA monitor, track action items for next quarter.

entries.title: Transfer Season Coordination (Apr-Jun)
entries.content: The busiest three months of the year for manning coordination — approximately 60-70% of all PCS moves in your OccField happen between April and June. Preparation starts in March: review the assignment plan (developed by the monitor in Jan-Feb) for wrong-grade fills or deprioritized key billets, flag your top 10 most critical fills with the monitor (deploying unit shortfalls, instructor billets, key leadership positions), proactively contact gaining command G-1s to confirm they are tracking inbound personnel, and align with TECOM on schoolhouse graduation dates to avoid off-cycle PCS. During transfer season expect near-daily contact with the monitor: weekly review of PCS order status, identify and resolve mismatches, and manage divert requests based on OccField impact. Common disruptions include medical holds (track weekly, advocate for expedited processing), dwell time waivers (support the monitor's justification), school seat conflicts (sequence school attendance and PCS appropriately), and command retention requests (default to the assignment plan unless compelling operational reason). Post-season in July: compile metrics (fill plan execution rate, disruptions, fill percentage achieved), brief the Branch Chief, feed lessons learned into next year's planning, and begin pre-coordination with the monitor immediately.

entries.title: Never Skip the Staffing Cycle
entries.content:
## What Happened
In 2024, my predecessor attempted to expedite a MOS Manual change — a modification to the prerequisites for MOS 0659 — by going directly from the draft stage to publication, bypassing the formal M&RA and TECOM coordination. The rationale was that the change was non-controversial and everyone had already agreed informally. The predecessor had verbal agreement from the monitor and the TECOM POC that the change was acceptable. The draft was submitted in October 2024 and sent directly to the Publications office in November. During the CMC Staff review (the final gate before publication), the change was flagged as unconcurred because neither M&RA nor TECOM had formal concurrence on file. When M&RA was asked for formal concurrence after the fact, the monitor's boss — a branch head who had not been consulted — identified a manning impact the monitor had not assessed. M&RA formally non-concurred. The change had to restart from the beginning of the staffing cycle. Total time lost: 6 months. The change that was supposed to be in the FY25 MOS Manual update was pushed to FY26.

## The Lesson
The staffing cycle exists for a reason and the shortcut is always longer. Informal agreement is not concurrence — a verbal "sure, that looks fine" from a working-level POC does not bind their organization; only a formal concurrence memo signed by the appropriate authority counts. Skipping steps creates enemies — M&RA and TECOM felt blindsided, and the working relationship was damaged for months. Rebuilding that trust cost far more time than the staffing cycle would have taken. Six months lost versus 8 weeks of normal staffing — the math speaks for itself.

## How to Apply This
Pre-coordinate informally to build support and identify objections early, then run the formal staffing cycle — every step, every signature, no exceptions. If you need to accelerate the timeline, call each stakeholder personally and ask them to prioritize your package; that is the legitimate way to move faster. Never skip a step and assume it will be fine, even when a change seems obvious and non-controversial. The formal process protects you as much as it protects the stakeholders: if something goes wrong after implementation, you need that concurrence trail to show you followed the process.

entries.title: TFDW Data Lags Reality by 60-90 Days
entries.content:
## What Happened
TFDW (Total Force Data Warehouse) is the authoritative system of record for Marine Corps manpower data, but it aggregates from multiple source systems (MCTFS, TFSMS, unit diary entries), each with its own update cadence, and the aggregation pipeline introduces additional delay.
